item.title: TF-M vs TF-A — When Do You Reach For Which?
item.description: TF-M is for Cortex-M microcontrollers, TF-A is for Cortex-A application processors. Different exception models, different attack surfaces, not interchangeable.

item.title: Do I Need a Secure Element If My MCU Has a Crypto Accelerator?
item.description: Short answer: they solve different problems. A crypto accelerator makes crypto operations fast. A secure element makes sure your keys never leave the chip.

item.title: Is SysTick Accurate Enough for Microsecond Timing?
item.description: Short answer: the counter is. Everything around it usually isn't.

item.title: What TrustZone-M Adds to Your Build
item.description: Why moving from a plain Cortex-M to a TrustZone-M part doubles your project structure — and what each new piece is actually doing.

item.title: Why Does My Code Work in Debug But Break in Release?
item.description: Short answer: optimization isn't doing the wrong thing. Your code was always wrong — debug builds just happened to hide it.

item.title: Hardware Root of Trust: What It Actually Means
item.description: Hardware Root of Trust is one of the most-used and least-understood terms in embedded security. A short, practical breakdown of what it is, what it isn't, and how to tell whether a product genuinely has one.

item.title: Why Your Secure Boot Probably Isn't Actually Secure
item.description: Most secure boot implementations look fine on paper but fail under realistic threat models. Eight common pitfalls — and what fixing them requires.

item.title: What Actually Happens Before main() Runs?
item.description: A lot. The CPU walks through six setup stages between power-up and your first line of C — and bugs in any of them look very different from each other.

item.title: About
item.description:
I’m Somesh Singh — an embedded software engineer specializing in hardware-rooted security for connected devices.

My work focuses on ARM Cortex-M and Cortex-A platforms, Trusted Firmware and TrustZone architectures, and building production-grade reference designs that global OEMs adopt for their own products. In practice, that means secure boot chains, signed FOTA update systems, hardware Secure Element integration, and the symmetric and asymmetric cryptography — AES, ECDH, ECC, RSA, TLS — that holds it all together.
